question:
Let $q$ and $r$ be integers with $q > 0$, and let $A$ and $B$ be intervals on the real line.
Let $T$ be the set of all $b+mq$ where $b$ and $m$ are integers with $b$ in $B$,
and let $S$ be the set of all integers $a$ in $A$ such that $ra$ is in $T$. Show that if the
product of the lengths of $A$ and $B$ is less than $q$, then $S$ is the intersection of $A$
with some arithmetic progression.
solution:
We begin with an easy lemma.
\setcounter{lemma}{0}
\begin{lemma*}
Let $S$ be a finite set of integers with the following property: for all $a,b,c \in S$ with
$a \leq b \leq c$, we also have $a+c-b \in S$. Then $S$ is an arithmetic progression.
\end{lemma*}
\begin{proof}
We may assume $\# S \geq 3$, as otherwise $S$ is trivially an arithmetic progression.
Let $a_1, a_2$ be the smallest and second-smallest elements of $S$, respectively, and put
$d = a_2 - a_1$. Let $m$ be the smallest positive integer such that $a_1 + md \notin S$.
Suppose that there exists an integer $n$ contained in $S$ but not in
$\{a_1, a_1 + d, \dots, a_1 + (m-1)d\}$, and choose the least such $n$.
By the hypothesis applied with $(a,b,c) = (a_1, a_2, n)$, we see that $n-d$ also has the property,
a contradiction.
\end{proof}

We now return to the original problem.
By dividing $B, q, r$ by $\gcd(q,r)$ if necessary, we may reduce to the case where $\gcd(q,r) = 1$.
We may assume $\#S \geq 3$, as otherwise $S$ is trivially an arithmetic progression.
Let $a_1, a_2, a_3$ be any three distinct elements of $S$, labeled so that $a_1 < a_2 < a_3$,
and write $ra_i = b_i + m_i q$ with $b_i, m_i \in \ZZ$ and $b_i \in B$. Note that $b_1, b_2, b_3$ must also be distinct, so the differences $b_2 - b_1, b_3 - b_1, b_3 - b_2$ are all nonzero; consequently, two of them have the same sign. If $b_i - b_j$ and $b_k - b_l$ have the same sign, then
we must have
\[
(a_i - a_j)(b_k - b_l) =  (b_i - b_j)(a_k - a_l)
\]
because both sides are of the same sign, of absolute value less than $q$,
and congruent to each other modulo $q$. In other words, the points $(a_1, b_1), (a_2, b_2), (a_3, b_3)$
in $\RR^2$ are collinear.
It follows that $a_4 = a_1 + a_3 - a_2$ also belongs to $S$ (by taking $b_4 = b_1 + b_3 - b_2$),
so $S$ satisfies the conditions of the lemma. It is therefore an
arithmetic progression.

\noindent
\textbf{Reinterpretations.}
One can also interpret this argument geometrically using cross products (suggested by Noam Elkies),
or directly in terms of congruences (suggested by Karl Mahlburg).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.} The problem phrasing is somewhat confusing: to say that ``$S$ is the intersection of
[the interval] $A$ with an arithmetic progression'' is the same thing as saying that ``$S$ is the empty set or an arithmetic progression'' unless it is implied that arithmetic progressions are necessarily infinite.
Under that interpretation, however, the problem becomes false; for instance, for
\[
q=5, r=1, A = [1,3], B = [0,2],
\]
we have
\[
T = \{\cdots, 0,1,2,5,6,7,\dots\}, S = \{1,2\}.
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Let $quasar$ and $radius$ be integers with $quasar > 0$, and let $intervala$ and $intervalb$ be intervals on the real line.
Let $settarget$ be the set of all $bravoid+muonid quasar$ where $bravoid$ and $muonid$ are integers with $bravoid$ in $intervalb$,
and let $setarith$ be the set of all integers $avalon$ in $intervala$ such that $radius avalon$ is in $settarget$. Show that if the
product of the lengths of $intervala$ and $intervalb$ is less than $quasar$, then $setarith$ is the intersection of $intervala$
with some arithmetic progression.
descriptive_long.solution:
We begin with an easy lemma.
\setcounter{lemma}{0}
\begin{lemma*}
Let $setarith$ be a finite set of integers with the following property: for all $avalon, bravoid, charlie \in setarith$ with
$avalon \leq bravoid \leq charlie$, we also have $avalon+charlie-bravoid \in setarith$. Then $setarith$ is an arithmetic progression.
\end{lemma*}
\begin{proof}
We may assume \# setarith \geq 3, as otherwise setarith is trivially an arithmetic progression.
Let $aoneval, atwoval$ be the smallest and second-smallest elements of $setarith$, respectively, and put
$deltaid = atwoval - aoneval$. Let $muonid$ be the smallest positive integer such that $aoneval + muonid deltaid \notin setarith$.
Suppose that there exists an integer $november$ contained in $setarith$ but not in
$\{aoneval, aoneval + deltaid, \dots, aoneval + (muonid-1)deltaid\}$, and choose the least such $november$.
By the hypothesis applied with $(avalon, bravoid, charlie) = (aoneval, atwoval, november)$, we see that $november-deltaid$ also has the property,
a contradiction.
\end{proof}

We now return to the original problem.
By dividing $intervalb, quasar, radius$ by $\gcd(quasar,radius)$ if necessary, we may reduce to the case where $\gcd(quasar,radius) = 1$.
We may assume \#setarith \geq 3, as otherwise setarith is trivially an arithmetic progression.
Let $aoneval, atwoval, athreev$ be any three distinct elements of $setarith$, labeled so that $aoneval < atwoval < athreev$,
and write $radius aelement = belement + melement quasar$ with $belement, melement \in \ZZ$ and $belement \in intervalb$. Note that $boneval, btwoval, bthreev$ must also be distinct, so the differences $btwoval - boneval, bthreev - boneval, bthreev - btwoval$ are all nonzero; consequently, two of them have the same sign. If $belement - belement$ and $belement - belement$ have the same sign, then
we must have
\[
(aelement - aelement)(belement - belement) =  (belement - belement)(aelement - aelement)
\]
because both sides are of the same sign, of absolute value less than $quasar$,
and congruent to each other modulo $quasar$. In other words, the points $(aoneval, boneval), (atwoval, btwoval), (athreev, bthreev)$
in $\RR^2$ are collinear.
It follows that $afourva = aoneval + athreev - atwoval$ also belongs to $setarith$ (by taking $b_4 = boneval + bthreev - btwoval$),
so $setarith$ satisfies the conditions of the lemma. It is therefore an
arithmetic progression.

\noindent
\textbf{Reinterpretations.}
One can also interpret this argument geometrically using cross products (suggested by Noam Elkies),
or directly in terms of congruences (suggested by Karl Mahlburg).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.} The problem phrasing is somewhat confusing: to say that ``$setarith$ is the intersection of
[the interval] $intervala$ with an arithmetic progression'' is the same thing as saying that ``$setarith$ is the empty set or an arithmetic progression'' unless it is implied that arithmetic progressions are necessarily infinite.
Under that interpretation, however, the problem becomes false; for instance, for
\[
quasar=5, \; radius=1, \; intervala = [1,3], \; intervalb = [0,2],
\]
we have
\[
settarget = \{\cdots, 0,1,2,5,6,7,\dots\}, \quad setarith = \{1,2\}.
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $dreamland$ and $blacksmith$ be integers with $dreamland > 0$, and let $riverbank$ and $farmhouse$ be intervals on the real line.
Let $driftwood$ be the set of all $shipwreck+snowflake dreamland$ where $shipwreck$ and $snowflake$ are integers with $shipwreck$ in $farmhouse$,
and let $cornfield$ be the set of all integers $sunflower$ in $riverbank$ such that $blacksmith sunflower$ is in $driftwood$. Show that if the
product of the lengths of $riverbank$ and $farmhouse$ is less than $dreamland$, then $cornfield$ is the intersection of $riverbank$
with some arithmetic progression.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We begin with an easy lemma.
\setcounter{lemma}{0}
\begin{lemma*}
Let $cornfield$ be a finite set of integers with the following property: for all $sunflower,shipwreck,tortoise \in cornfield$ with
$sunflower \leq shipwreck \leq tortoise$, we also have $sunflower+tortoise-shipwreck \in cornfield$. Then $cornfield$ is an arithmetic progression.
\end{lemma*}
\begin{proof}
We may assume $\# cornfield \geq 3$, as otherwise $cornfield$ is trivially an arithmetic progression.
Let $heliotrope, thumbtack$ be the smallest and second-smallest elements of $cornfield$, respectively, and put
$lighthouse = thumbtack - heliotrope$. Let $snowflake$ be the smallest positive integer such that $heliotrope + snowflake lighthouse \notin cornfield$.
Suppose that there exists an integer $raspberry$ contained in $cornfield$ but not in
$\{heliotrope, heliotrope + lighthouse, \dots, heliotrope + (snowflake-1)lighthouse\}$, and choose the least such $raspberry$.
By the hypothesis applied with $(sunflower,shipwreck,tortoise) = (heliotrope, thumbtack, raspberry)$, we see that $raspberry-lighthouse$ also has the property,
a contradiction.
\end{proof}

We now return to the original problem.
By dividing $farmhouse, dreamland, blacksmith$ by $\gcd(dreamland,blacksmith)$ if necessary, we may reduce to the case where $\gcd(dreamland,blacksmith) = 1$.
We may assume $\#cornfield \geq 3$, as otherwise $cornfield$ is trivially an arithmetic progression.
Let $heliotrope, thumbtack, goldcrest$ be any three distinct elements of $cornfield$, labeled so that $heliotrope < thumbtack < goldcrest$,
and write $blacksmith\,teapotlid = windstorm + scarecrow\,dreamland$ with $windstorm, scarecrow \in \ZZ$ and $windstorm \in farmhouse$. Note that $raincloud, starlight, moonbeams$ must also be distinct, so the differences $starlight - raincloud, moonbeams - raincloud, moonbeams - starlight$ are all nonzero; consequently, two of them have the same sign. If $windstorm - b_j$ and $b_k - b_l$ have the same sign, then
we must have
\[
(teapotlid - a_j)(b_k - b_l) =  (windstorm - b_j)(a_k - a_l)
\]
because both sides are of the same sign, of absolute value less than $dreamland$,
and congruent to each other modulo $dreamland$. In other words, the points $(heliotrope, raincloud), (thumbtack, starlight), (goldcrest, moonbeams)$
in $\RR^2$ are collinear.
It follows that $parchment = heliotrope + goldcrest - thumbtack$ also belongs to $cornfield$ (by taking $b_4 = raincloud + moonbeams - starlight$),
so $cornfield$ satisfies the conditions of the lemma. It is therefore an
arithmetic progression.

\noindent
\textbf{Reinterpretations.}
One can also interpret this argument geometrically using cross products (suggested by Noam Elkies),
or directly in terms of congruences (suggested by Karl Mahlburg).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.} The problem phrasing is somewhat confusing: to say that ``$cornfield$ is the intersection of
[the interval] $riverbank$ with an arithmetic progression'' is the same thing as saying that ``$cornfield$ is the empty set or an arithmetic progression'' unless it is implied that arithmetic progressions are necessarily infinite.
Under that interpretation, however, the problem becomes false; for instance, for
\[
dreamland=5, blacksmith=1, riverbank = [1,3], farmhouse = [0,2],
\]
we have
\[
driftwood = \{\cdots, 0,1,2,5,6,7,\dots\}, cornfield = \{1,2\}.
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $infinitum$ and $noninteger$ be integers with $infinitum > 0$, and let $singleton$ and $singular$ be intervals on the real line.
Let $emptiness$ be the set of all $externalval+divisorval\,infinitum$ where $externalval$ and $divisorval$ are integers with $externalval$ in $singular$, and let $continuum$ be the set of all integers $irrational$ in $singleton$ such that $noninteger\,irrational$ is in $emptiness$. Show that if the product of the lengths of $singleton$ and $singular$ is less than $infinitum$, then $continuum$ is the intersection of $singleton$ with some arithmetic progression.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
We begin with an easy lemma.
\setcounter{lemma}{0}
\begin{lemma*}
Let $continuum$ be a finite set of integers with the following property: for all $irrational,externalval,decimalval \in continuum$ with $irrational \leq externalval \leq decimalval$, we also have $irrational+decimalval-externalval \in continuum$. Then $continuum$ is an arithmetic progression.
\end{lemma*}
\begin{proof}
We may assume $\# continuum \geq 3$, as otherwise $continuum$ is trivially an arithmetic progression.
Let $terminusone, terminustwo$ be the smallest and second-smallest elements of $continuum$, respectively, and put $summative = terminustwo - terminusone$. Let $divisorval$ be the smallest positive integer such that $terminusone + divisorval\,summative \notin continuum$.
Suppose that there exists an integer $continuous$ contained in $continuum$ but not in $\{terminusone, terminusone + summative, \dots, terminusone + (divisorval-1)summative\}$, and choose the least such $continuous$. By the hypothesis applied with $(irrational,externalval,decimalval) = (terminusone, terminustwo, continuous)$, we see that $continuous-summative$ also has the property, a contradiction.
\end{proof}

We now return to the original problem. By dividing $singular, infinitum, noninteger$ by $\gcd(infinitum,noninteger)$ if necessary, we may reduce to the case where $\gcd(infinitum,noninteger) = 1$.
We may assume $\#continuum \geq 3$, as otherwise $continuum$ is trivially an arithmetic progression.
Let $terminusone, terminustwo, terminusthree$ be any three distinct elements of $continuum$, labeled so that $terminusone < terminustwo < terminusthree$, and write $noninteger\,aggregate = outervalue + staticindex\,infinitum$ with $outervalue, staticindex \in \ZZ$ and $outervalue \in singular$.
Note that $outerone, outertwo, outerthree$ must also be distinct, so the differences $outertwo - outerone, outerthree - outerone, outerthree - outertwo$ are all nonzero; consequently, two of them have the same sign.
If $outervalue - outervalue$ and $outervalue - outervalue$ have the same sign, then
\[
(aggregate - aggregate)(outervalue - outervalue) =  (outervalue - outervalue)(aggregate - aggregate)
\]
because both sides are of the same sign, of absolute value less than $infinitum$, and congruent to each other modulo $infinitum$.
In other words, the points $(terminusone, outerone), (terminustwo, outertwo), (terminusthree, outerthree)$ in $\RR^2$ are collinear.
It follows that $terminusfour = terminusone + terminusthree - terminustwo$ also belongs to $continuum$ (by taking $b_4 = outerone + outerthree - outertwo$), so $continuum$ satisfies the conditions of the lemma. It is therefore an arithmetic progression.

\noindent\textbf{Reinterpretations.} One can also interpret this argument geometrically using cross products (suggested by Noam Elkies), or directly in terms of congruences (suggested by Karl Mahlburg).

\noindent\textbf{Remark.} The problem phrasing is somewhat confusing: to say that ``$continuum$ is the intersection of [the interval] $singleton$ with an arithmetic progression'' is the same thing as saying that ``$continuum$ is the empty set or an arithmetic progression'' unless it is implied that arithmetic progressions are necessarily infinite. Under that interpretation, however, the problem becomes false; for instance, for
\[
infinitum=5, noninteger=1, singleton = [1,3], singular = [0,2],
\]
we have
\[
emptiness = \{\cdots, 0,1,2,5,6,7,\dots\}, \; continuum = \{1,2\}.
\]

garbled_string.question:
Let $pznemuka$ and $ktovxird$ be integers with $pznemuka > 0$, and let $bsukojzm$ and $ezrgalyp$ be intervals on the real line.
Let $lqenkatz$ be the set of all $hjgrksla+srbpjutapznemuka$ where $hjgrksla$ and $srbpjuta$ are integers with $hjgrksla$ in $ezrgalyp$,
and let $dhvyxqpl$ be the set of all integers $qzxwvtnp$ in $bsukojzm$ such that $ktovxirdqzxwvtnp$ is in $lqenkatz$. Show that if the
product of the lengths of $bsukojzm$ and $ezrgalyp$ is less than $pznemuka$, then $dhvyxqpl$ is the intersection of $bsukojzm$
with some arithmetic progression.
garbled_string.solution:
We begin with an easy lemma.
\setcounter{lemma}{0}
\begin{lemma*}
Let $dhvyxqpl$ be a finite set of integers with the following property: for all $qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,mfldqvro \in dhvyxqpl$ with
$qzxwvtnp \leq hjgrksla \leq mfldqvro$, we also have $qzxwvtnp+mfldqvro-hjgrksla \in dhvyxqpl$. Then $dhvyxqpl$ is an arithmetic progression.
\end{lemma*}
\begin{proof}
We may assume $\# dhvyxqpl \geq 3$, as otherwise $dhvyxqpl$ is trivially an arithmetic progression.
Let $yjgcfqwa, qermlvds$ be the smallest and second-smallest elements of $dhvyxqpl$, respectively, and put
$plxtrnqy = qermlvds - yjgcfqwa$. Let $srbpjuta$ be the smallest positive integer such that $yjgcfqwa + srbpjutaplxtrnqy \notin dhvyxqpl$.
Suppose that there exists an integer $vekldspi$ contained in $dhvyxqpl$ but not in
$\{yjgcfqwa, yjgcfqwa + plxtrnqy, \dots, yjgcfqwa + (srbpjuta-1)plxtrnqy\}$, and choose the least such $vekldspi$.
By the hypothesis applied with $(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,mfldqvro) = (yjgcfqwa, qermlvds, vekldspi)$, we see that $vekldspi-plxtrnqy$ also has the property,
a contradiction.
\end{proof}

We now return to the original problem.
By dividing $ezrgalyp, pznemuka, ktovxird$ by $\gcd(pznemuka,ktovxird)$ if necessary, we may reduce to the case where $\gcd(pznemuka,ktovxird) = 1$.
We may assume $\#dhvyxqpl \geq 3$, as otherwise $dhvyxqpl$ is trivially an arithmetic progression.
Let $yjgcfqwa, qermlvds, bhzaikng$ be any three distinct elements of $dhvyxqpl$, labeled so that $yjgcfqwa < qermlvds < bhzaikng$,
and write $ktovxird xzmpfela = gkrohvye + zvqntdsapznemuka$ with $gkrohvye, zvqntdsa \in \ZZ$ and $gkrohvye \in ezrgalyp$. Note that $kwfetajz, vqsnrwly, mdsqpkhg$ must also be distinct, so the differences $vqsnrwly - kwfetajz, mdsqpkhg - kwfetajz, mdsqpkhg - vqsnrwly$ are all nonzero; consequently, two of them have the same sign. If $gkrohvye - gkrohvye$ and $gkrohvye - gkrohvye$ have the same sign, then
\[
(xzmpfela - xzmpfela)(gkrohvye - gkrohvye) =  (gkrohvye - gkrohvye)(xzmpfela - xzmpfela)
\]
because both sides are of the same sign, of absolute value less than $pznemuka$,
and congruent to each other modulo $pznemuka$. In other words, the points $(yjgcfqwa, kwfetajz), (qermlvds, vqsnrwly), (bhzaikng, mdsqpkhg)$
in $\RR^2$ are collinear.
It follows that $uitrxsop = yjgcfqwa + bhzaikng - qermlvds$ also belongs to $dhvyxqpl$ (by taking $b_4 = kwfetajz + mdsqpkhg - vqsnrwly$),
so $dhvyxqpl$ satisfies the conditions of the lemma. It is therefore an
arithmetic progression.

\noindent
\textbf{Reinterpretations.}
One can also interpret this argument geometrically using cross products (suggested by Noam Elkies),
or directly in terms of congruences (suggested by Karl Mahlburg).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.} The problem phrasing is somewhat confusing: to say that ``$dhvyxqpl$ is the intersection of
[the interval] $bsukojzm$ with an arithmetic progression'' is the same thing as saying that ``$dhvyxqpl$ is the empty set or an arithmetic progression'' unless it is implied that arithmetic progressions are necessarily infinite.
Under that interpretation, however, the problem becomes false; for instance, for
\[
pznemuka=5, ktovxird=1, bsukojzm = [1,3], ezrgalyp = [0,2],
\]
we have
\[
lqenkatz = \{\cdots, 0,1,2,5,6,7,\dots\}, dhvyxqpl = \{1,2\}.
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let p and s be positive integers and let
  I = (\alpha , \alpha  + L] and J = [\beta , \beta  + M)
be two half-open real intervals whose (positive) lengths L and M satisfy
    L\cdot M < p .
Define
  U = { j + k p : j \in  \mathbb{Z} \cap  J , k \in  \mathbb{Z} },   R = { n \in  \mathbb{Z} \cap  I : s n \in  U } .
Prove that R is the intersection of I with an arithmetic progression; that is, show that there exist integers n_0 and d \geq  0 such that
    R = I \cap  { n_0 + k d : k \in  \mathbb{Z} } .
(When d = 0 the progression consists of the single point n_0; in particular, if that single point lies outside I then the intersection - and hence R - is empty.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout, ``interval'' means a subset of \mathbb{R}, while ``arithmetic progression'' means a subset of \mathbb{Z} (finite when d = 0, infinite when d > 0).


0.  A preparatory reduction
Put g = gcd(p, s) and set p' = p/g, s' = s/g.  For integers n, j we have
  s n \equiv  j (mod p) \Leftrightarrow  s' n \equiv  j/g (mod p').
Hence the congruence is solvable only when g | j.  Define
  J' = { x/g : x \in  J },  U' = { j' + k p' : j' \in  \mathbb{Z} \cap  J' , k \in  \mathbb{Z} } .
Then
  R = { n \in  \mathbb{Z} \cap  I : s' n \in  U' }.                                (1)
Because L\cdot (M/g) = L\cdot M' < p', it suffices to prove the required statement in the coprime case gcd(p, s) = 1.  Henceforth we assume
  gcd(p, s) = 1 and L\cdot M < p .


1.  A combinatorial lemma
Lemma.  Let S \subset  \mathbb{Z} be finite and satisfy
 (\star ) for all a \leq  b \leq  c in S we have a + c - b \in  S.
Then S is an arithmetic progression.

Proof.  If |S| \leq  2 the claim is obvious.  Otherwise list the elements in increasing order
  a_1 < a_2 < \cdots  < a_m  (m \geq  3)
and put d := a_2 - a_1.  Choose the least k (1 \leq  k \leq  m - 1) with a_1 + k d \notin  S and put n := a_1 + k d.  Let a_j be the least element of S that is \geq  n.  Applying (\star ) with (a,b,c) = (a_1,a_j,n) gives n - d \in  S, contradicting minimality of k.  Hence no such k exists and S = {a_1 + t d : 0 \leq  t \leq  m - 1}.  \square 


2.  First easy cases
If \mathbb{Z} \cap  J = \emptyset  then U = \emptyset , hence R = \emptyset .  Choosing any integer n_0 outside I and taking d = 0 gives I \cap  {n_0} = \emptyset  = R, so the theorem holds.

Next suppose \mathbb{Z} \cap  J contains exactly one integer, say j_0.  Then every n \in  R satisfies s n \equiv  j_0 (mod p).  Because gcd(s,p)=1 this is equivalent to n \equiv  n_0 (mod p), where n_0 is the unique residue with s n_0 \equiv  j_0 (mod p).  Thus
  R = I \cap  { n_0 + k p : k \in  \mathbb{Z} },
and the theorem is proved.  Henceforth we assume
  |\mathbb{Z} \cap  J| \geq  2 .                                                (2)


3.  Three elements of R and some bounds
Choose three distinct elements n_0 < n_1 < n_2 of R.  Write
  s n_i = b_i + m_i p  with b_i \in  \mathbb{Z} \cap  J,  m_i \in  \mathbb{Z}.            (3)
Because (2) and gcd(s,p)=1 imply L < p (indeed, M \geq  1 so L\cdot M < p \Rightarrow  L < p), the congruences (3) show that two equal residues b_i would force n_i = n_j; therefore
  b_0, b_1, b_2 are pairwise distinct.                            (4)
For any i \neq  j we have |n_i - n_j| < L and |b_i - b_j| < M, hence
  |(n_i - n_j)(b_k - b_\ell )| < L\cdot M < p.                         (5)


4.  A determinant which is a multiple of p
For distinct indices i,j,k set
  \Delta _{ijk} = (n_i - n_j)(b_k - b_j) - (b_i - b_j)(n_k - n_j).
Because s is invertible modulo p, (3) gives
  \Delta _{ijk} \equiv  0  (mod p).                                      (6)
Bounding |\Delta _{ijk}|.  Since the three n's lie in an interval of length L and the three b's in an interval of length M, the triangle with vertices (n_i, b_i) is contained in an axis-parallel rectangle of width L and height M, so
  |\Delta _{ijk}| = 2\cdot (area of that triangle) < L\cdot M < p.            (7)
Combining (6) and (7) we get \Delta _{ijk} = 0.


5.  A reference line containing ALL points of R
The vanishing of \Delta _{012} shows that (n_0, b_0), (n_1, b_1), (n_2, b_2) are collinear.  Denote by \ell  this line.  We next show that every point (n,b) arising from an element n \in  R also lies on \ell .

Fix the two indices 0 and 1.  For an arbitrary n \in  R write s n = b + m p with b \in  \mathbb{Z} \cap  J.  Form
  \Delta (n) := (n_0 - n_1)(b - b_1) - (b_0 - b_1)(n - n_1).
Exactly as in Section 4, the two factors on the left are < L and < M in absolute value, so |\Delta (n)| < L\cdot M < p, while
  \Delta (n) \equiv  (s n_0 - s n_1)(s^{-1} b - s^{-1} b_1) - (b_0 - b_1)(n - n_1) \equiv  0 (mod p),
using that s is invertible modulo p.  Hence \Delta (n) = 0, which means (n,b) lies on \ell .

Consequently, for every three elements a \leq  b \leq  c of R the corresponding points are collinear, and so the midpoint reflection a + c - b in that line produces yet another integer point of \ell .  Because the b-coordinate of that point is \beta ' = (b_a + b_c - b_b) which still lies in J (J has length M and the three b's are contained in it), we have a + c - b \in  R.  Thus R satisfies property (\star ).


6.  Finishing the argument
Since I is bounded, R is a finite set of integers.  If |R| \leq  2 we are done.  Otherwise R is finite and satisfies (\star ); by the lemma of Section 1 it is an arithmetic progression.  Because every element of that progression lying in I belongs to R (by construction of R), we finally have
  R = I \cap  { n_0 + k d : k \in  \mathbb{Z} }
for suitable integers n_0 and d \geq  0, completing the proof. \square

core_steps: Lemma: a finite integer set closed under a+c−b is an arithmetic progression.
Make r and q coprime (divide by gcd); assume |S| ≥ 3.
For any three a_i∈S, write r a_i = b_i (mod q) with b_i∈B; small interval lengths give |a_i−a_j||b_k−b_l| < q.
That bound plus the congruence forces (a_i−a_j)(b_k−b_l) ≡ (b_i−b_j)(a_k−a_l) (mod q) ⇒ collinearity ⇒ a₁+a₃−a₂ ∈ S.
Apply the lemma to conclude S is the intersection of A with an arithmetic progression.

slot1.description: Topological type of the intervals (open, closed, half-open) and their exact positions on the real line; only their lengths enter the argument.
slot1.original: A and B are (unspecified) intervals on ℝ

slot2.description: The particular triple of distinct elements chosen from S to invoke collinearity; any three distinct elements would work.
